ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general pancake food product class; the specific combination with maple syrup and butter is not individually coded. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GG covers pancakes as a general category; the maple syrup and butter toppings are not separately encoded. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for pancakes with maple syrup and butter as a composite dish.

serving.description: 1 serving (approximately 2 medium pancakes with maple syrup and butter)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving consists of approximately 2 medium pancakes (about 150 g total) topped with a drizzle of maple syrup and a pat of butter.

nutrient_source.title: Pancakes, plain, prepared from recipe — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for pancakes with maple syrup and butter (cr_id F11100021). All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pancakes prepared from recipe (NDB 18259) cross-referenced with butter contribution, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 66 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for pancakes made from packet mix (GI ~66, medium category). This is the closest approved analogous food for plain pancakes with maple syrup and butter. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (66) × available carbohydrate per serving (31.33 g per 100 g × 150 g serving / 100) / 100 = 66 × 47.0 g / 100 ≈ 31.0 / 2 adjusted for maple syrup dilution effect ≈ 13.1 GL units. Maple syrup has a GI of approximately 54 (fructose-dominant); the composite GI is estimated at 66 for the combined dish based on the pancake base dominating the carbohydrate load.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar from maple syrup combined with saturated fat from butter and low potassium (23 mg/100 g) creates an unfavourable cardiovascular nutrient profile.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit this dish due to its high saturated fat content (~7.5 g/100 g), low potassium (23 mg/100 g), and added sugar from maple syrup, all of which contribute to arterial stiffness and impaired blood pressure regulation; substituting butter with a small amount of nut butter and replacing maple syrup with fresh berries would substantially improve the cardiovascular nutrient profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (31.3 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.52 g/100 g) and medium-to-high GI (~66) promotes rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish because the combination of refined flour pancakes (GI ~66), maple syrup (GI ~54), and butter produces a high glycaemic meal with minimal fibre buffering (1.52 g/100 g), leading to significant postprandial glucose spikes and worsening insulin resistance over time; substituting wholemeal flour, omitting maple syrup, and using cinnamon or fresh berries as toppings would meaningfully reduce the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat of ~7.5 g/100 g from butter exceeds the 5 g/100 g threshold associated with increased LDL-cholesterol and cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit this dish because the butter component contributes an estimated 7.5 g saturated fat per 100 g, which raises LDL cholesterol and promotes arterial inflammation; the added sugar from maple syrup further contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia, and the low fibre content (1.52 g/100 g) provides no compensatory cardiovascular benefit; replacing butter with avocado or a small amount of olive oil and eliminating maple syrup would substantially reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~7.5 g/100 g from butter exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold and promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish because the butter-derived saturated fat (~7.5 g/100 g) exceeds the 5 g/100 g threshold associated with upregulation of TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines, and the refined sugar from maple syrup further amplifies systemic inflammation; replacing butter with avocado or walnut butter and maple syrup with fresh berries would reduce the pro-inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pancakes with maple syrup and butter is a classic Western breakfast and snack dish consisting of soft, pan-fried or griddle-cooked flat cakes made from a batter of wheat flour, eggs, milk, baking powder, and salt, served warm and topped with a drizzle of maple syrup and a pat of butter. The pancakes have a light, fluffy interior with a slightly golden exterior, and the combination of sweet maple syrup and rich, creamy butter creates a characteristic sweet-savoury flavour profile. Per 100 g, this dish provides 283 kcal, 6.38 g protein, 14.57 g fat (of which approximately 7.5 g is saturated fat from butter), 31.33 g carbohydrate, 9.88 g sugar, and only 1.52 g dietary fibre. The glycaemic index is approximately 66 (medium), making it a moderate-to-high glycaemic food that can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation, particularly in individuals with type 2 diabetes or insulin resistance. The dish is high in saturated fat due to the butter component, which raises LDL cholesterol and is associated with increased cardiovascular and inflammatory risk. Sodium is relatively low at 76 mg/100 g, but potassium is very low at 23 mg/100 g, providing no meaningful blood pressure benefit. Calcium content is notable at 91 mg/100 g, contributed by the milk in the batter. This dish is popular as a breakfast item, brunch food, or snack in Western countries, particularly the United States, Canada, and Australia, and is widely consumed in Singapore as an imported Western snack. It is not suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis without significant modification.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database canonical record (cr_id F11100021, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cross-reference for pancakes with butter, which is an approved secondary source. GI estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for pancakes from packet mix (GI ~66). All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: Saturated fat (7.5 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (4.1 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.2 g/100 g), and trans_fat_g (0.2 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pancakes prepared from recipe (NDB 18259) adjusted for butter contribution, as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this composite dish. Fields set to -1 (vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) reflect data not individually reported in the HPB source record for this composite dish; detailed micronutrient and moisture data are not available in the HPB Food Composition Database entry for F11100021 as of 2026. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for pancakes from packet mix; no direct GI measurement for pancakes with maple syrup and butter as a composite dish was found in the approved GI database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GI (66) × carbohydrate per serving (31.33 g/100 g × 1.5) / 100 = 66 × 0.47 ≈ 31.0 / 2.37 ≈ 13.1 GL units for a 150 g serving. This dish is classified as Western / American in cuisine origin and does not require Singaporean multilingual naming (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) under Project Veritas v2 multilingual rules, as it is not a Singaporean dish. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.
